prompt: Design a Kanban board for a support team handling 50 tickets/week across L1 triage, L2 investigation, and L3 engineering escalation.
assertions: board includes distinct columns for each support tier (L1, L2, L3) plus queue/buffer columns between them
WIP limits are assigned to every active-work column
swimlanes differentiate by severity or service class (e.g., expedite vs. standard)
entry and exit criteria are defined for each column transition
includes a blocker/escalation zone for aged tickets
card template includes required fields: ticket ID, severity, age-in-column, assignee, blocker flag
output includes a Mermaid diagram of the board layout

prompt: Calibrate WIP limits for a software team with 6 developers and 2 testers. Current average cycle time is 12 days, throughput is 8 items/week, and average WIP is 14.
assertions: applies Little's Law explicitly: Lead Time = WIP / Throughput
identifies that current WIP of 14 is likely too high given the team size and cycle time
recommends per-column WIP limits, not just a system-wide number
accounts for the tester bottleneck (2 testers vs. 6 developers) in limit distribution
includes sensitivity analysis with at least 2 scenarios (tight vs. balanced)
defines re-calibration triggers with specific thresholds
predicts cycle time improvement after WIP reduction

prompt: Our team's average cycle time has doubled from 5 days to 10 days over the last 6 weeks. Diagnose the flow bottleneck and recommend fixes.
assertions: requests or analyzes cumulative flow diagram data to identify widening bands
checks WIP trends to determine if WIP increased (overloading) or throughput decreased (capacity loss)
identifies the specific column or stage where items are accumulating
analyzes blocker frequency and duration as a potential root cause
provides at least 2 concrete intervention recommendations tied to the diagnosed bottleneck
uses percentile-based cycle time analysis (P50, P85, P95) rather than just averages
tags findings with evidence markers: [METRIC] for data-backed, [INFERENCIA] for hypotheses

prompt: Implement service classes for a team that handles both BAU production support (30 tickets/week) and strategic project work (3-4 features/sprint).
assertions: defines at least 3 service classes with distinct cost-of-delay profiles
includes an Expedite class with strict WIP limit (max 1 item) for critical production incidents
sets allocation percentages that balance BAU and project work without starving either
defines aging thresholds per service class based on cycle time percentiles
includes a classification flowchart or decision tree for incoming work
specifies Service Level Expectations (SLEs) as probabilistic commitments (not guarantees)
addresses conflict resolution when multiple expedite items arrive simultaneously
